funFact: Their wide-set eyes grant 360° vertical vision — they can see directly above and below simultaneously, giving them an almost complete view of their surroundings.
description: Named for the scalloped front edge of its distinctive hammer-shaped head (cephalofoil), this species aggregates in huge schools during the day — a rare social behavior among sharks. Their populations have collapsed by over 80% in recent decades, driven almost entirely by the fin trade.
habitat: Tropical and warm temperate seas worldwide; coastal, estuarine, and semi-oceanic

vitalSigns.label: Reproductive Rate
vitalSigns.value: 15–31 pups per litter; ~9–10 month gestation; biennial cycle

vitalSigns.label: Diet
vitalSigns.value: Bony fish, squid, octopus, and crustaceans

vitalSigns.label: Population Growth Rate
vitalSigns.value: Very low — late female maturity limits recovery

vitalSigns.label: Key Senses
vitalSigns.value: Cephalofoil provides 360° vertical vision and enhanced electroreception via expanded Ampullae of Lorenzini

populationTrendMeta.note: Absolute numbers are modeled. The shape of the decline is well-supported: multiple independent studies document 80–89% collapses in NW Atlantic and Indo-Pacific subpopulations since the 1980s (IUCN 2008 CR uplisting).

preyDeclineRegions.name: Indo-Pacific
preyDeclineRegions.note: Reef fish and squid stocks severely depleted by overfishing

preyDeclineRegions.name: Gulf of Mexico
preyDeclineRegions.note: Coastal fish populations under heavy trawling and longline pressure

preyDeclineRegions.name: N. Pacific
preyDeclineRegions.note: Tuna and squid populations reduced by commercial fleets

preyDeclineRegions.name: Mediterranean
preyDeclineRegions.note: Fish stocks depleted; limited hammerhead range

preyDeclineRegions.name: Red Sea
preyDeclineRegions.note: Reef fish under pressure from local and commercial fishing

preyDeclineRegions.name: E. Pacific
preyDeclineRegions.note: Some prey populations more stable near upwelling zones

fishingPressureRegions.name: Indo-Pacific
fishingPressureRegions.note: Largest fin trade hub; daytime aggregation schools targeted directly

fishingPressureRegions.name: Gulf of Mexico
fishingPressureRegions.note: Heavy longline bycatch in tuna and swordfish fisheries

fishingPressureRegions.name: Mediterranean
fishingPressureRegions.note: Longline and gillnet fleets; minimal enforcement

fishingPressureRegions.name: E. Pacific
fishingPressureRegions.note: Bycatch in tuna purse seine fisheries

fishingPressureRegions.name: Red Sea
fishingPressureRegions.note: Targeted fishing at smaller scale; some artisanal take

healthMetrics.label: Habitat Quality
healthMetrics.value: Degraded — coastal nursery estuaries under heavy pressure

healthMetrics.label: Fishing Pressure
healthMetrics.value: Critical — fin trade and surface-school bycatch

healthMetrics.label: Protection Coverage
healthMetrics.value: Partial — CITES Appendix II; some national bans on finning

threats.name: Shark Fin Trade
threats.description: The scalloped hammerhead's distinctive fins are among the most prized in the trade. Demand has driven an 80%+ population collapse, with targeted finning the primary driver of decline.

threats.name: Bycatch
threats.description: Caught incidentally in tuna and swordfish longline fisheries at extremely high rates. Schools near the surface make them especially vulnerable during daytime aggregations.

threats.name: Coastal Habitat Loss
threats.description: Pups and juveniles rely on shallow estuaries and coastal bays as nursery habitat. Urban development, mangrove loss, and pollution degrade these critical areas.

threats.name: Climate Change
threats.description: Warming seas compress preferred temperature bands and shift prey distributions, putting additional pressure on already fragmented populations.

actionItems.title: Refuse Shark Fin Products
actionItems.description: Scalloped hammerhead fins are among the most traded in the global fin trade. Refusing to buy or consume shark fin soup and related products directly reduces demand driving their decline.

actionItems.title: Urge Stronger CITES Enforcement
actionItems.description: The scalloped hammerhead is listed on CITES Appendix II, but trade continues illegally. Contact your government representatives to push for stronger enforcement and expanded trade restrictions.

actionItems.title: Support Hammerhead Conservation Research
actionItems.description: Donate to organizations like the Shark Trust and Save Our Seas Foundation that fund population research and advocate for stronger protections for this critically endangered species.

actionItems.title: Choose Sustainable Seafood
actionItems.description: Bycatch in tuna and swordfish fisheries is the second major driver of decline. Use the Monterey Bay Aquarium Seafood Watch to avoid seafood caught with methods that incidentally kill hammerheads.

actionItems.title: Protect Coastal Nursery Habitats
actionItems.description: Support organizations and policies that protect mangroves, estuaries, and coastal bays — the nursery areas juvenile hammerheads depend on. Coastal habitat loss is an underappreciated threat to recovery.
